title: Training Methodology Candidates To Add
description: The human-enablement dimension: methodologies for training people to use, automate with, build with, and build on AI/agents. Distinct from the methodologies collection (which constructs AI systems). Candidates are teachable Moves located on the Craft Path, evidenced by real-world Known Uses. Built from training-todo-src/*.json shards via _build_training_todo.py.

lexicon.craft-path: The road a learner walks from beginner to expert, made of ordered Steps.
lexicon.step: A stage on the Craft Path, named by the stance the learner occupies.
lexicon.move: A single reusable way of teaching — the atomic building block.
lexicon.methodology-pattern: A Move written up in full (intent, steps, diagram, example).
lexicon.track: An orchestrated program that chains Patterns up the Craft Path.
lexicon.threshold: The test that earns the next Step.
lexicon.masterpiece: The real piece of work shown to prove a Step was earned.
lexicon.craft: The skill being built — the AI Fluency 4D set.
lexicon.container: The shape of the session (workshop, clinic, bootcamp, cohort, hackathon, dojo, retreat, coaching).
lexicon.mode: How learning happens (concept, hands-on, bring-your-own-problem, mentored-unblock, pair-cohort, capstone).
lexicon.reach: The unit of change (individual to ecosystem).
lexicon.guardrail: Cross-cutting safety lens applied at every Step.
lexicon.acculturation: The foundation layer beneath Operator: build shared understanding of WHY before teaching HOW.

relationship_to_methodologies.rule: Training raises a LEARNER's capability through experience and practice. The learner can be a human OR an autonomous agent (e.g. Sparrot). A methodology is the PROCESS an already-capable actor follows to build or run a system. Sorting test: finishing it produces a more capable learner -> Training; a built or improved artifact -> Methodology.
relationship_to_methodologies.decision: Kept as two separate dimensions, linked. Each Craft Path Step lists unlocks_methodologies: the methodology categories a graduate at that Step is now equipped to execute.
relationship_to_methodologies.model_training_note: Model/LLM training (pretraining, fine-tuning, RLHF) is METHODOLOGY, not Training. It is a build/improve-the-system process and stays in the methodologies collection. The Training dimension is EXPERIENTIAL upskilling only (workshops, practice, mentoring, drills) — never weight-level changes.
relationship_to_methodologies.agent_learner_note: Learners and trainers may be autonomous agents. An agent like Sparrot can be trained experientially (attend workshops, drill, consolidate memory, rehearse tool use) and can also deliver Moves (run workshops for humans or other agents). See the learner/trainer facets and any agent-specific Moves.

craft_path.goal: Understand why AI matters (acculturation) before touching tools.
Use AI/LLM tools for daily work.
Change how a team or company works, using agents.
Start a new business run by agents.

aliases: AIエージェントハッカソン
candidates.summary: A time-boxed intensive (48–72 hours, or a structured 5-day sprint) where mixed teams build and ship a working automation or tool by the end. Mentors float to unblock; the output is a live tool plus shared prompts, not a slide deck.
mechanics: Pre-assign teams of 4–5 by job function; include at least one technical participant per team.
Set clear problem space before the event: identify automation gaps in current workflows.
Use alternating workshop and build days (e.g. Workshop Wednesday's Mon/Wed/Fri + Tue/Thu build rhythm).
Provide API keys, compute credits, and starter templates to remove setup friction.
Mentor-to-team ratio of 1 pair per 2–3 teams.
Demo day: each team presents a working tool; judges evaluate on practical business value over technical complexity.
Winners' prompts and automation blueprints go into the shared library.
proof: Each team ships a deployed, working automation by the final day.
Post-event: at least one tool is in active use the following week.
Prompt blueprints from winning solutions are added to the team's shared library.

candidates.name: Checkpoint-Gated Spike
candidates.summary: A strictly time-boxed (typically 1-3 day) experiment whose deliverable is knowledge, not shipped code: when facing an unknown — e.g. whether an LLM can do a task reliably — the team runs a small experiment with a defined question and a checkpoint at the end of the box, rather than speculating or open-ended researching. It pays off by converting uncertainty into a decision while capping the investment.
mechanics: Name the specific question, risk, or uncertainty to resolve
Set a hard time box (commonly 1-3 days) and a fixed budget
Run a small, almost throwaway experiment — prototype, probe, or research
At the time-box checkpoint, present findings: decision, proof of concept, or estimate
Either proceed with confidence or split the question into smaller spikes
Capture the learning so it informs the real story (the spike output is knowledge)
proof: SAFe defines the deliverable as risk reduction: gaining the knowledge necessary to reduce the risk of a technical approach or increase estimate reliability.
Mountain Goat: because spikes are time-boxed, the investment is fixed — so a failed exploration costs a bounded, known amount.

known_uses.name: Spikes (Scaled Agile Framework)
known_uses.quote: When faced with a question, risk, or uncertainty, Agile Teams conduct small experiments before moving to implementation rather than speculate about the outcome.

known_uses.name: Spikes (Mountain Goat Software / Mike Cohn)
known_uses.quote: With a spike, a team isn't trying to immediately deliver a new capability; instead, they are building the knowledge that will allow them to deliver the new capability later.

sources.title: Spikes - Scaled Agile Framework
sources.quote: Spikes represent activities such as exploration, architecture, infrastructure, research, design, and prototyping.

sources.title: What Are Agile Spikes? When and Why Do Teams Use Spikes?
sources.quote: In agile projects, a spike refers to a time-boxed research activity that helps teams make better decisions & deliver better products.

candidates.name: Show the Working
candidates.summary: A training module that teaches builders to surface agent reasoning and action traces to end users — via logs, step-by-step summaries, or 'explain-then-act' patterns — so that users can verify agent behaviour and trust the output enough to adopt it.
mechanics: Explain that over 65% of organisations cite 'lack of explainability' as the primary barrier to AI adoption
Demonstrate the 'explain-then-act' pattern: agent outputs reasoning before executing an action
Builders instrument an existing agent with a tracing tool (e.g. OpenTelemetry, Langfuse, LangSmith)
Practise writing human-readable step summaries that a non-technical user can review
Show the three log layers: context (what the agent knew), reasoning (why it chose), actions (what it did)
Optional: add a human-in-the-loop approval gate for high-stakes actions
proof: The agent built by the participant exposes a readable trace or step summary to the end user, not just the final answer
User acceptance test: a non-technical tester can read the trace and confirm whether the agent behaved correctly

candidates.name: Teach the Failure Modes
candidates.summary: A training session that drills builders on where agents break in production — tool hallucination, instruction drift, infinite loops, non-idempotent replay, metrics blindness — so they design for failure from the start rather than discovering it after deployment.
mechanics: Enumerate the canonical failure modes (tool hallucination, instruction drift, infinite loops, over-eager execution, false success, state desync, flaky tools, non-idempotent replay, permission creep, silent degradation, metrics blindness)

candidates.name: Action Learning Set
candidates.summary: A small standing group (a set) meets regularly; each member brings a real, unsolved problem (e.g. an AI adoption or automation challenge), gets uninterrupted airtime, and the others advance it through questioning rather than advice, followed by reflection and committed action before the next meeting. Revans captured it as L = P + Q: programmed knowledge plus questioning.
mechanics: Form a small set of peers (typically 5-8) who are each working on a genuine, current problem
Give one member at a time uninterrupted time to present their challenge
The rest help mainly by asking open, probing questions rather than dispensing solutions
The presenter commits to concrete actions before the next meeting (no learning without action)
Members return, report what happened, and reflect on results to extract learning
A facilitator holds the process (or the set self-facilitates once mature)
proof: Revans' principle: there can be no learning without action, and no action without learning.
Organisations adopting action-learning — Oxfam, Heineken, EY — report improved cross-functional working, ownership and progress against objectives.

candidates.name: Trust via Transparency
candidates.summary: Require AI tools and agents to display their reasoning or evidence — not just their answer — so that staff learn to verify outputs rather than blindly accept them. This is an org-level guardrail, not just a UX feature: the policy that says 'agents must explain, humans must check'.
mechanics: Set a policy: any AI output used in a decision must show a source, a confidence signal, or a chain-of-thought trace that a domain expert can verify.

Train staff on the two-pathway model: transparency increases perceived effectiveness (→ trust) but can also reveal discomfort (→ distrust) — so teams need to learn what good explanations look like, not just demand them.
Include 'verify the citation' as a mandatory step in any AI-assisted workflow checklist.
Run tabletop exercises: present an AI output with no explanation vs. one with a source trace; discuss which decision the team would stake their name on.
Track downstream error rate and human-override rate as Masterpiece metrics.
proof: Yu & Li (2022, Behavioral Sciences, n=235): AI transparency increases perceived effectiveness (promotes trust) AND increases discomfort (inhibits trust) via parallel mediation — 'AI decision-making transparency (vs. non-transparency) led to higher perceived transparency, which in turn increased both effectiveness (which promoted trust) and discomfort (which inhibited trust)'. Both effects are real and must be managed.
McKinsey 2025: 'those that fail to build trust will miss the opportunity to deliver on AI's full potential for their customers and employees'.
XAI market reached $9.77B in 2025 (CAGR 20.6%); orgs with explainable AI achieve 30% higher ROI than black-box implementations.

candidates.name: 4D Fluency Framework

candidates.name: Anti-pattern: Vibe-Ship Without Review

mechanics: Symptom: participant ships multiple apps per day without reading a single line of generated code.
